commentBody: Wow, so cool!
Quite the tag team for a tricky Sunday! Quite a bit to C here; I hope people are Open to it (and don't consider it ANTIART). I'm glad you're not the AWAYTEAM, Rachel and Adam. NOICE!
I needed help to CLOSE THE LOOP on this one, but “O” wow does it pay off when you “C” the clues transform from nonsensical to perfectly reasonable when you remove the BLOCKADE. Kudos!

Remember a few days ago, I said that if a puzzle had too much going on, the payoff had better be worth it?

Here's that puzzle. I got some of the upper theme answers without knowing why. With trick puzzles like these, I just plunk in what's obviously the answer with a few letters remaining, and move on. I completed, for example, YOUTHFUL INDISCRETIONS with about half the letters, all the while not knowing why it matched [Wild cats]. When LATE NIGHT was the answer for [Arsenic's realm, in years past], I realized they really mean Arsenio, and I had to change all the C's in the theme clues to O's. But I still didn't know why.

And then there was the matter of the three BLOCKs.  Obviously, here we had something different:  a two-part, slash-separated clue for each. The dark squares seemed to be BLOCKs, and as such, the answer pairs, such as ICE BLOCK and BLOCKers, solved the clues.

But why?  You had to look at the grid once all of that was filled in (or if solving electronically, wait for the animation). The big C in the middle becomes an O with the three BLOCK rebuses returning to actual BLOCKs, as they were in the clue. (The only thing I wonder is how many solvers know that black grid squares are called BLOCKs.)

Or, of course, you can look at 113A.

And for the cherry on top, there are no C's in any of the clues except those that have to change, and no O's except those in your head after you make the  C-to-O changes.

This puzzle might be a Puzzle of the Year contender, IMHO.

AWOL and desertion are not synonyms. A soldier who is AWOL is simply not at his place of duty. A deserter is an absentee who intends to remain absent *permanently.* Each has its own article in the UCMJ: desertion comes under Article 85, AWOL under Article 86. I prepared the court-martial charge sheet for one soldier who was AWOL for five minutes — he missed bed check — and I know of a case of one absentee who was tried and acquitted of a charge of desertion after an absence of 20 years.
I completely missed that there were no Os or Cs in any of the non-theme clues. THAT'S as big a challenge as creating the delightful grid. I'm floored.

I almost protested at “Alfa,” but thankfully did a little
research. I did not know “alfa” was acceptable (or even preferred) in lieu of “alpha” in the NATO alphabet.

Learn something new every day (and every puzzle).

Relatively late comment from me since I was traveling last night but not a lot to add to the well deserved adulation this brilliantly executed multilayered puzzle is getting. I didn’t even notice the lack of O’s or C’s in the other clues which speaks to the consummate skill of these clues—a unnecessary restriction even, at least for the O’s , since the theme could be all C’s in the clues become O’s but that doesn’t mean there can’t be other O’s. So bravo for that.
One more observation from a construction standpoint was how clever the grid construction was and what it must have taken to find such brilliant theme material that fits it symmetrically. I am most awed that the two best themes are grid spanners and how much luck, talent, or both does it take to find two like this of 21 letters exactly??!!
If I had any minor disappointment it would reflect an occasional Sunday pitfall based on titles. The title prepared me for theme and I got it pretty quickly with MINIMALLS which made many of the other theme answers practically gimmes. Imagine if this were like a big Thursday without  a title, how delicious the puzzle would have been, pretty much bamboozled most of the way through, or at least further along, until more of the theme dawned on me. I know some folks try not to look at the title on Sundays but with the new changes on the App it pops up pretty prominently, and often is required (or has other notes) that are necessary to understand the theme.

This is my favorite kind of theme: multilayered and hard to figure out, but never unfair. No hand-holding here, and I loved it! I didn't notice that none of the non-theme clues had an O or a C in them until after I'd read the column. That just makes this puzzle more amazing!

Arsenic/Arsenio is what clued me in to the theme. Though admittedly at first I thought arsenic might be used for a LATE NIGHT poisoning. Dastardly deeds always happen at night, right? The center trick fell into place thanks to the "naval barrier" clue--for some reason that word has been on my mind a lot lately.

I had King Kong instead of LEAR for the longest time. I really did not want to let go of that gorilla.

I guess I’m the outlier: I really didn’t love this one. It felt like a masterful (and it is masterful!) construction at the expense of, rather than in service of a good solving experience.

I ended up doing this quite rapidly without ever sorting out the theme: just plugged in answers to so-so clues and fit phrases to the italicized clues. Only when I was done did I see what it all meant. Looking back, I see an impressive feat, but I can’t say it was much fun getting there.
Gotta love a graphic revealer!

After two crunchy puzzles in a row, I was expecting to be disappointed.  And, at first, my expectations weren't disappointed.  My first impressions were kinda "meh".
But, WAIT!  There's a twist at the end!

When I saw the clue "Wild cats" in italics, I thought, "That's a clever clue," but then I thought, "Hmm, it's only one letter away from 'Wild oats'."  I looked at the other italicized clues.  "Large cutlets"?  Oh, c'mon.  That's obviously "large outlets", and when that quickly solved to MEGA-MALL, my disappointment grew.

"Arsenic" = "Arsenio"
"Chic" = "Ohio"
Is that all you got???
And the revealer solved too easily to CLOSE THE LOOP.
Yeah, yeah, the C's are O's; I get it.
I had already seen the big C in the middle of the puzzle.  And I had noticed how the C destroyed the symmetry that we expect in a grid.  
Too big a price, it had seemed to me, for a weak gimmick.

But then I tackled the heart of the puzzle: the middle of the C, and my whole assessment changed.
I saw all those "slash" clues, and I couldn't quite figure them out.  I could get one half, but not the other:  ICE BLOCK and BLOCKADE, for example.  And I couldn't seem to make them fit.
Rebuses to the rescue!
Every puzzle is made better by a rebus, or three!  
And that solved the mystery of the strange clue at 65-Down.

And then the cherry on top!  The lovely animation.
And with it the grid's symmetry is restored!!!
Now that's both beautiful and clever.
Well done,  Rachel Fabi and Adam Wagner!
A great puzzler!

Yowza! Such a brilliant piece of work.

Why has ADNOUN become obsolete?

Court-martial charge sheet for a missed bed check, Fact Boy? I hope that was resolved with, at most, an Article 15.

@Fact Boy

Is AWOL a lesser included offense? Such that a deserter is AWOL, but going AWOL doesn’t always amount to desertion.

He was charged with malingering, wrongful urination, and two counts of AWOL. Acquitted of malingering and wrongful urination, he was sentenced to 6 months in the stockade for the AWOL. On release, he was reassigned to a unit in West Germany, got as far as Fort Dix, waited three days without being shipped, gave up and went home to drive a taxicab. 579 days later, he reported back to Fort Dix, demanding a discharge, since he figured his time was up. They tried to court-martial him on an AWOL charge, but his unit in Europe had failed to acquire him on their Morning Report, so they couldn't prove the charge. The Army administratively added 579 days to his enlistment and reassigned him to Fort Hamilton in Brooklyn. He sued the Army in federal court demanding his release — he was represented by William Kunstler's firm, and the lawsuit got a big article in the NY Times — but the court found his story not credible. I was stuck in SW Oklahoma and would have sued the Army to trade places with him. A pillar of his community in Michigan, he died a couple of years ago in his eighties.

Being AWOL is never desertion. For a charge of desertion to stick, the prosecution has to show the intent to remain absent permanently. In the case I mentioned — not my experience; I read about it in the Army Times — the defense argued that since the defendant had kept is DD Form 2A (military ID card) and dog tags, he had always intended to return to duty, and he was acquitted, even after a 20-year absence.

I'd been AWOL one lonnng weekend during my first enlistment back in 1977 from Ft Ord. I was an 11B at the time, and our reasoning was, "What're they gonna do to us, change our MOS to 11B and put us in the Infantry?"

I was on a weekend pass in CT when one of my buddies came down with meningitis. Grace New Haven hospital called our company at Fort Dix to say that we were being held overnight for observation. In one bar, we ran into an off-duty MP from the Brooklyn Army Terminal who treated us to drinks in exchange for our story. @lucky13 You will occasionally see some trickery (usually of the mild type) on Sundays, and Thursday is the main day for shenanigans and chicanery. When you're new to the crosswords, it's very helpful to read the Wordplay column, one of several helpful columns that appear at https://www.nytimes.com/column/wordplay. In today's case, a C is unclosed circle (more or less). To fully understand the clues, you need to change those Cs to Os, "closing the loop" or "come full circle". So Arsenic becomes Arsenio (Hall, a former late night talk show host). The only Cs in the clues appear in the theme clues, and they all need to be changed to Os to get the correct answers. Rebuses (more than one letter in a square) can happen on any day, but they're not a usual occurrence, and there are articles that introduce them and how to enter them. Most of us who have been here a while enjoy the additional challenges of a puzzle that has its own rules every once in a while.

Teresa,
I suspect the editors did look it up. Euphemism has a broader definition than just replacing harmful words.

@Barry Ancona As a grammar and syntax specialist, I agree with Teresa. Yes, some people use “euphemism” to mean a replacement word for any kind of term (not only offensive or unpalatable ones). However, that’s not correct, and, as Teresa notes (and this clue demonstrates), it can be misleading to use it that way. I doubt many dads would prefer to be referred to as an “old block” than as “Dad” 😄

SB,
Prescriptivism is not a good look -- or a practical one -- in the Wordplay comments. If dictionaries record usage beyond replacement of harmful words, the usage is fair game for the Crossword. And while I seek to avoid misdirection when editing copy, I adore it in puzzles.

commentBody:
@Vogon
Two complications:
Rebus here does not mean what it means in the rest of the puzzling world. Here, a REBUS is the placement of more than one letter in a single square in the grid. In this puzzle,  all 5 letters of BLOCK fit into a single square, and this trick occurred 3 times.
But sometimes REBUS does mean what it does everywhere else -- the use of pictures to transmit words or letters that are built up into words, an ancient puzzle form. Thus: 🐝+🍂=Bee + Leaves = BELIEVES. Here, the clue we're given for each tricksy square is a ■ = BLOCK.
So this puzzle requires you to look at the picture, think 'Block' and then insert
B-L-O-C-K in that single square using the REBUS button. For some, that extra layer of puzzle-think is delightful. For some, it's annoying or even a betrayal of what the puzzle purports to be -- a set of words crossing.
I'm in the delighted group but there are plenty here who are not.
